transcript.clean_text: It's the moment we have all been anticipating since the Giro d'Italia route was announced early this year. Stage 11, the Strade Bianche stage to Montalcino. 162 km with 35 K's of Sterrato, the infamous white gravel road, all of them located in the final 70 K's of the race. Adding to the mix just under 2 and 1/2 thousand meters of climbing, mostly in the latter part of the day, and we had all the ingredients for a truly epic day in the saddle. Given the nature of it, we expected a big battle to get into the early break, but not only did it go quickly, it was also allowed almost 15 minutes of an advantage. Behind them, we did have the expected almighty fight for position going into the first gravel sector, and Ganna getting a little too excited on it, almost losing it on this right-hander with race leader Egan Bernal on his wheel. That pressure had had the desired effect on the rides behind them, though. Gaps opening up left, right, and center with Evenepoel amongst the big names on the wrong side of one. Two groups did come back together before the next sector, but both Dan Martin and Davide Formolo were absent. Fast forward 30 K's, and Evenepoel was in trouble again. The rider in second place on GC completely isolated, and Bernal did not need to hear that news twice. He was straight to the front of his group driving the pace. And the Belgian was furious about something, pulling out his earpiece as Almeida still hadn't come back to help him. At this point, we could see that the breakaway were fighting out for the stage victory. Just under 6 minutes advantage with 12 K's to go. A few K's further on, Emanuel Buchmann had gone on the attack. That acceleration enough to see Vincenzo Nibali dropped. With 5 K's to go, just two rides were left out front, Mauro Schmid and Alessandro Covi. As Buchmann pressed on behind them, Evenepoel had already lost close to 2 minutes on his main rivals, and Ciccone, too, was about to lose contract with the main group. One man, though, was better than all of the rest again today, and it was the pink jersey himself, Egan Bernal, flying past Vlasov and soon onto the back wheel of Buchmann. At the finish, we had a drag race between our two young riders out front. Schmid opening up first, Covi able to draw alongside him, but that was as far as he got. The Swiss rider from Qhubeka Assos taking a famous stage victory. After that, though, all eyes were on this man. How much time could Egan Bernal put into his rivals? He sprinted all the way up to the finish line, and we soon had our answer. 3 seconds before Buchmann crossed the line, and it would be 23 before this man, Aleksandr Vlasov, stopped the clock for Astana Premier Tech. Behind him, Caruso, Yates, and Foss were a further 3 seconds back, with Carthy suffering towards the finish, conceding 32 seconds on the pink jersey. For Evenepoel, it was a long and agonizing ride towards the finish line. 2 minutes and 8 seconds were what he conceded to Bernal. This was the man of the day, though. Hats off to Mauro Schmid. That was some ride for a first-year pro. And it was the breakaway who filled the top 10 slots on the day. Van Houcke in third place, then De Bondt, Guglielmi, Battaglin, Kluge, Gavazzi, Van der Hoorn, and Narvaez. Best of the rest was Bernal, and it was the GC favorites between 11th and 20th. Vlasov, Caruso, Yates, Foss, Guerrero, Carthy, Ciccone, and Bettiol there also for EF Education-Nippo. Well, it was a good day for this man. Egan Bernal now has a much more comfortable advantage on the GC, 45 seconds in front of Vlasov. Damiano Caruso now up to third at 1:12. Carthy in fourth at 1:17. Yates there in fifth. Buchmann up nine places to sixth, and Evenepoel down to seventh place at 2 minutes and 22. No rest for the wicked, though, as tomorrow is another demanding day for the riders. We're in the Apennines for what will be the longest stage of the race so far at 212 km. No gravel tomorrow, despite the fact that we start from Siena, but we do have some serious climbing to contend with. Four classified climbs, the biggest being the Passo della Consuma after 132 K's, and the Passo della Calla 30 K's later. The finish line is located in Bagno di Romagna, a venue that saw Omar Fraile take the win 4 years ago. It'll be somebody different tomorrow, though, so make sure you tune back into GCN or Eurosport to catch all the action.
